segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nyxara was already awake when the light arrived; warlocks don't court daylight -- they wait it out. She came down the loft's shadow-ladder of the stable and stopped in the dark beneath the last rafter, away from the door. The left glove came off.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She held the hand up in the thin grey light coming through the gap in the boards. The fingertips were black to the second knuckle now. The skin had the texture of something that had been cold too long -- not dead, not yet, but learning the direction.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She flexed each finger in turn, feeling the nerves report from their diminished distance. Not pain exactly. More like the memory of pain, arriving late and quiet. She counted the joints. Same number as yesterday.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She had stopped being grateful for that and started just counting.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The ring finger was the worst.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The black had climbed past the second knuckle to the base, and when she bent it, there was a fraction of a second's delay between the instruction and the obedience -- a tiny lag, like a conversation through a translator, where her body had once been a single language.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She'd noticed the delay three days ago. She hadn't told anyone. The delay was hers.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She tested the grip: fist closed, fist open, fist closed. Strength at maybe seventy percent. Enough for wards. Enough for the subtle work -- the suggestion-veils, the psychic caltrops she'd laid during the siege. Not enough for the deep casting, the patron's full gift. Not without losing more.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The math was simple and she'd done it many times. Each major casting cost a joint. A knuckle. An inch of herself converted to the patron's currency. At this rate, the hand by winter. The arm by spring. And after the arm --

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She didn't think past the arm. Past the arm was a conversation she wasn't ready to have.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The glove went back on before she stepped into the yard.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She crossed the square alone. The cobbles were still scorched in places where Caelin's fire had swept through; the barricade had been pulled aside but not yet cleared, and someone had left a bucket where a man had died.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The smell of the night before hung in the cold air -- pitch, iron, the strange wrong-sweetness the thralls left behind. She walked through it without slowing. She had learned early that the best way to move through a bad place was to move through it.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She found Caelin where she'd expected -- on the stable step in the early cold, carefully re-wrapping his arm, testing the grip against the ache. He didn't try to stand when she approached. It would cost too much.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A thumb-sized mirror-coin lay in her palm, frost veining the face from the inside.

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "Summoned,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "And I was told to tell you. You, specifically."

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "By your patron?"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He kept his eyes on the wrap.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She tilted the coin; the frost vanished as if swallowed.

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "When they call, I go. And right now I'm held together with spite and linen. If I cast like I did yesterday, I'll lose these."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She lifted the gloved hand. He knew what was under it.

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "Next time it's the hand. Then the arm."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He nodded once.

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "Do you want me to tell the others?"

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "In a bit."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She pressed a looped obsidian sliver into his palm -- smooth, black, the length of a thumbnail and sharp enough to draw blood from the air around it.

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "One breath where lies land like truth. Spend it to buy time, not fate."

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "What does that mean?"

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "It means you'll know when you need it. And when you use it, you'll wish you hadn't. That's how all the best gifts work."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Before he could answer, she drew out a narrow dark ribbon knotted with a hair-fine thread of silver.

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "Tie it under your sleeve. If I'm within about a hundred miles, the silver will shade darker. Not a map -- just nearness."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He studied it.

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "That helps."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She didn't add -- and he didn't ask -- that the knot carried a return thread only she could read; that if she needed to find him, the ribbon answered her too. Some gifts are best given without explaining the price tag.

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "Anything else I should know?"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he asked.

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "Only this."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She took his wrist, quick and careful, and tied the ribbon for him beneath the wrap where it wouldn't show. Her fingers -- even the dark ones -- moved with the precise grace of someone who'd learned that dexterity was not optional in her profession.

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "I'm going because I must, not because I want to. And -- "

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: her grin flashed, wicked even through exhaustion

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: " -- try not to exhaust yourself without me. I'd hate to miss it."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Even hurt, he huffed a laugh.

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "No promises."

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "Good boy."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Two fingers -- the least ruined -- touched his cheek, almost ceremonial. A blessing from a woman who didn't believe in blessings, offered to a man who didn't believe he deserved one.

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "Walk with ghosts if you must. Don't become one to keep them company."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She stepped away into the lane, her boots finding the cleanest path through the scorched cobbles without apparent effort. She didn't look back. She was good at not looking back. She'd had a lot of practice.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin watched her go. The yard felt larger -- the way a room feels larger when the heat has gone, when the particular energy of a person who takes up more space than their body occupies simply isn't there anymore.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He touched the ribbon under his wrap. The silver thread was cool. He wondered how far a hundred miles was and whether he'd feel the moment it went dark.
